name: Lauryn Hill
description:
'''Lauryn Noelle Hill''' (born May 26, 1975) is an American rapper, singer, and songwriter. She is considered one of the most influential musicians of her time. A definitive figure in [[neo soul]] and a pioneer of [[rap-singing]] and [[melodic rap]], ''[[The Telegraph (London)|The Telegraph]]'' credited Hill with the popularization of [[hip-hop]]. ''[[NPR]]'' named her one of the [[50 Great Voices]] and stated that her "voice is the story of the hip-hop generation". She was the only woman named on the lists of "Greatest MCs of All Time" (2006) by [[MTV]] and [[Billboard and Vibe's 50 Greatest Rappers of All Time|10 Greatest Rappers]] by [[Billboard (magazine)|''Billboard'']], and appeared on ''[[Rolling Stone]]''<nowiki/>'s [[Rolling Stone's 200 Greatest Singers of All Time|200 Greatest Singers]] and [[VH1]]'s [[The Greatest (VH1 TV series)|100 Women in Music]].

Hill began her career as a teen actress, portraying Kira Johnson in the [[soap opera]] ''[[As the World Turns]]'' (1991) before appearing in [[Steven Soderbergh]]'s film ''[[King of the Hill (1993 film)|King of the Hill]]'' (1993). She received acclaim for her role as Rita Watson in ''[[Sister Act 2: Back in the Habit]]'' (1993), which featured her rendition of "[[His Eye Is on the Sparrow]]." Hill achieved prominence as the frontwoman of the [[Fugees]]. Their second album, ''[[The Score (album)|The Score]]'' (1996), included the singles "[[Killing Me Softly with His Song|Killing Me Softly]]" and "[[Ready or Not (Fugees song)|Ready or Not]]," and sold over 22 million copies worldwide. It won the [[Grammy Award for Best Rap Album]], making Hill the first woman to receive the honor.

Her debut solo album, ''[[The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill]]'' (1998), debuted atop the [[Billboard 200|''Billboard'' 200]] with the highest first-week sales for a female artist at the time and became the first recording by a female rapper to be certified [[List of music recording certifications|diamond]]. Its lead single "[[Doo Wop (That Thing)]]" debuted at number one on the [[Billboard Hot 100|''Billboard'' Hot 100]], making Hill the first artist whose first appearance on both charts debuted at number one. The album produced several hits, including "[[Ex-Factor]]," "[[Everything Is Everything (Lauryn Hill song)|Everything Is Everything]]," and "[[Lost Ones (Lauryn Hill song)|Lost Ones]]." At the [[41st Annual Grammy Awards]], Hill became the first rapper to win [[Grammy Award for Album of the Year|Album of the Year]] and the first woman to win five awards in a single night. It remains one of the [[List of best-selling albums|best-selling albums]] worldwide and ranked atop [[Apple Music 100 Best Albums|Apple Music's 100 Best Albums]]. Afterwards, she embarked on [[The Miseducation Tour]] and became the first rapper to appear on the cover of [[Time (magazine)|''Time'']].

Around this period, Hill became a noted collaborator, featuring on "[[If I Ruled the World (Imagine That)]]" by [[Nas]], "[[Guantanamera]]" by [[Wyclef Jean]] with [[Celia Cruz]], "[[The Sweetest Thing (Refugee Camp All Stars song)|The Sweetest Thing]]" by [[Refugee Camp All-Stars]], and the virtual duet "[[Turn Your Lights Down Low]]" with [[Bob Marley]]. She wrote "[[A Rose Is Still a Rose (song)|A Rose Is Still a Rose]]" by [[Aretha Franklin]], and produced recordings for [[Whitney Houston]], [[Mary J. Blige]], and [[Santana (band)|Santana]]. Her live album ''[[MTV Unplugged No. 2.0]]'' (2002), featured original songs such as "[[I Gotta Find Peace of Mind]]" and "[[Mystery of Iniquity]]", the latter of which was notably [[Interpolation (popular music)|interpolated]] on "[[All Falls Down]]" by [[Kanye West]]. In the years that followed, Hill made fewer public appearances while occasionally releasing acclaimed music like the [[Black Lives Matter]] protest song "[[Black Rage (song)|Black Rage]]" and the Nas collaboration "[[Nobody (Nas and Lauryn Hill song)|Nobody]]".

Hill is the recipient of [[List of awards and nominations received by Lauryn Hill|numerous accolades]] including eight [[Grammy Awards]] (the [[Grammy Award milestones|most for any female rapper]]), the inaugural [[BET Awards#Special awards|BET Living Legend Icon Award]], the [[American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers|ASCAP]] Golden Note Award, four [[NAACP Image Awards]] (including the [[NAACP Image Award – President's Award|President's Award]]), and six [[MTV Video Music Awards]] (including [[MTV Video Music Award for Video of the Year|Video of the Year]]), with inductions into the [[Grammy Hall of Fame]], the [[National Recording Registry]], and the [[Black Music & Entertainment Walk of Fame]]. She is one of the highest-grossing rappers in live music. In 2026, she was nominated for the [[Rock and Roll Hall of Fame]].
